From the Desert to the Hills: Rajasthan and MP Students Begin 10-Day Cultural Odyssey in the Northeast

‘Ashtalakshmi Darshan’: 44-Member Team from MP and Rajasthan Arrive at Assam University to Uncover the ‘True Essence’ of the Northeast

AU
Assam University Media Cell 28 March 2026

A Group Photo of Members from MP, Rajasthan and Assam University

To strengthen national unity and cultural integration, a youth team of 44 members arrived at Assam University, Silchar campus on Friday, 27 March 2026, for the ‘Ashtalakshmi Darshan’ programme, a joint initiative of the North Eastern Council and Assam University. Led by four coordinators, 40 students from Rajasthan and Madhya Pradesh will gather firsthand experience of this region over the next ten days. The primary goal of this programme is to highlight the spirit of unity within India’s diversity.

On Friday, Hon’ble Vice-Chancellor of Assam University, Prof. Rajive Mohan Pant welcomed the youth team at the university guest house conference hall. Distinguished geologist Prof. Trilochan Singh was present as the chief guest. Honored guests included Prof. Alok Tripathi, former Additional Director General of the Archaeological Survey of India, and Prof. Ramaiya Balakrishnan, Dean of the School of Educational Sciences and Director of the Madan Mohan Malaviya Mission Teacher Training Center.

Vice-Chancellor Prof. Pant stated that ‘Atithi Devo Bhava’ (The guest is God) is the core foundation of North East India’s hospitality. He shared the historical context, noting that the concept of ‘Ashtalakshmi’ gained popularity after Sikkim became part of India in 1975. It is to be noted that the Prime Minister of India, Shri Narendra Modi often refers to the eight states of the Northeast as the ‘Ashtalakshmi’ or the eight pillars of India’s prosperity.

Lamp lighting ceremony — Ashtalakshmi Darshan Youth Exchange Programme

Prof. Trilochan Singh shared his travel experiences in the Northeast during the 1990s and his time spent in Arunachal Pradesh. He inspired the youth by narrating the story of a thrilling road expedition across 16 countries from India to South Africa.

Prof. Alok Tripathi, while describing the tales of bravery from North East India, cited examples from the Mahabharata. He specifically mentioned the courage of King Bhagadatta of Pragjyotishpur.

Prof. Ramaiya Balakrishnan discussed the natural resources of Assam and the ‘Indianness’ deeply embedded in the local culture.

The Nodal Officer of the programme and Professor at the Department of Social Work, Prof. M. Gangabhushan stated that the students might have to stay in conditions without electricity or modern facilities. This is intended so they can set aside urban comforts and get close to the soil to properly realise the true essence of this region.

From Madhya Pradesh, Dr. Neeraj Singh Gurjar and Dr. Shalini Kaushik, and from Rajasthan, Dr. Rijushna Gonge and postgraduate student Vikash Bali, are serving as coordinators for this programme.
